planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, peat or coir-based, slightly acidic (pH 6.0–6.5) soil in deep shade to bright indirect light; calathea is sensitive to fluoride, chlorine, and salt in water — use rainwater or filtered water for best results. Never expose to direct sun, which bleaches and burns the foliage.

directions: Plant in moist, peat-based, slightly acidic soil in bright indirect shade; calatheas are among the most spectacularly decorative foliage plants in the world, producing large, rounded or lance-shaped leaves with intricate patterns of dark green, silvery green, and cream in bold graphic patterns that look almost painted. C. orbifolia in particular produces enormous round leaves with elegant silver stripes. The foliage of most calatheas moves with the light — leaves rise in the evening and lower in the morning in a circadian rhythm that gives the related marantas their name 'prayer plants.' In zone 10–12 calatheas thrive as outdoor shade garden plants under tree canopy; in zone 9a they are outstanding patio container plants in bright, indirect shade. They require consistently moist (but never waterlogged) soil, high humidity, and protection from drafts and direct sun.

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in deep to moderate shade; peace lily requires warmth (above 60°F) and protection from direct sun and drying drafts. It is toxic to cats and dogs — the calcium oxalate crystals cause mouth irritation if ingested.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil in deep shade; peace lily is one of the most adaptable, forgiving, and widely grown of all tropical shade plants — equally at home as an outdoor ground cover in zone 10–12 deep shade and as a low-light houseplant in virtually any indoor setting. It produces broad, glossy, dark green leaves and distinctive white spathes (modified leaves) surrounding a cream-colored spadix from spring through summer, and often reblooms in fall. It is one of very few flowering plants that tolerates genuine deep shade with limited light, making it indispensable for covered patios, north-facing rooms, and the deepest shade under trees. Water when the top inch of soil feels dry; the plant will droop dramatically when too dry but recovers quickly after watering. A NASA Clean Air Study plant found to remove formaldehyde, benzene, and trichloroethylene from indoor air.
sources: NASA Clean Air Study (Wolverton et al., 1989)

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, peat-based, slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in bright indirect shade; maranta requires consistent moisture, high humidity, and warmth above 60°F. Avoid hard water — use rainwater or allow tap water to sit 24 hours before using to allow chlorine to off-gas.

directions: Plant in moist, slightly acidic, peat-based soil in bright indirect shade; the prayer plant is named for its remarkable circadian leaf movement — the broad, oval leaves with their intricate dark green and silver herringbone pattern are flat during the day but fold upward in the evening in a prayer-like posture, then return to horizontal at dawn. This nyctinasty (night-time leaf folding) is especially dramatic in the variety 'Erythroneura,' which has brilliant red veins against velvety dark green with light green feathering — one of the most beautiful leaf patterns of any plant. As an outdoor shade plant in zone 10–12, maranta spreads as a low groundcover under trees and in shaded planting beds. Indoors it thrives in any room with bright indirect light. Keep soil consistently moist and mist frequently or place on a pebble tray with water to maintain humidity.

common_name: Night-Blooming Cereus (Queen of the Night, Dutchman's Pipe Cactus)

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic cactus or orchid bark mix in bright indirect shade; despite being a cactus, this epiphytic species grows naturally in tropical forest canopies and prefers shade and higher humidity than desert cacti. Allow the cut end to callus 1–2 days before planting.

directions: Root cuttings in well-drained cactus or orchid bark mix in bright indirect shade; night-blooming cereus is one of nature's most theatrical performances — the enormous, pure white, intensely fragrant flowers 10–12 inches across bloom only after dark, open for a single night, and wilt before dawn. The flowers, which appear irregularly in summer and fall, often cause garden 'watching parties' when buds are spotted, as the opening in real-time over a few hours is mesmerizing. Unlike most cacti, this epiphytic cactus from tropical forests grows best in partial shade and moist, well-drained organic soil rather than full sun and dry conditions. The flat, leaf-like stems are unremarkable until they flower. In zone 9a it is an outstanding container plant kept on a shaded porch, brought indoors before frost. The fruit is edible and sweet.

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, warm, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after last frost and when soil reaches 60°F; black turtle beans perform as either bush or climbing types depending on variety. For dry bean production, harvest when 90% of pods have turned brown and dry.

directions: Sow 1–2 inches deep in warm, well-drained soil in full sun; black turtle beans are the standard black bean of Latin American, Caribbean, and American cuisine — the dense, shiny black seeds are rich in protein (15g per cup) and antioxidant anthocyanins, used in black bean soup, rice and beans, burritos, tacos, and countless other dishes. Both bush and pole varieties are available; the most common home-garden varieties include 'Black Valentine' (bush) and 'Black Coco' (bush). Allow pods to dry completely on the plant for dry bean harvest — seeds should rattle in the pod. Thresh by spreading dried plants on a tarp and walking on them, then winnow. In zone 9a, plant in spring for a late-summer dry bean harvest; the plants fix atmospheric nitrogen and improve soil for subsequent crops.

common_name: Navy Bean (Haricot Bean, Boston Bean, White Pea Bean)

planting.note: Direct sow 1–2 inches deep in well-drained, warm, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun after last frost; navy beans are compact bush types that set pods heavily in a concentrated period. In zone 9a they perform best with spring planting for early summer harvest before peak summer heat.

directions: Sow 1–2 inches deep in warm, well-drained soil in full sun; navy beans are the small, oval, ivory-white dried beans of New England baked beans, Senate bean soup, and countless European cassoulet and bean dishes. The compact bush plants are highly productive and concentrate their pod set for a single, efficient harvest period — more practical for dry bean production than many spreading pole types. Allow pods to turn yellow-brown and papery before harvest; pull the entire plant and dry in a warm, well-ventilated location for 1–2 weeks, then shell. Navy beans are rich in fiber (19g per cup) and folate. In zone 9a, spring plantings avoid the hottest summer weather; flowers drop in temperatures above 90°F, so early planting is key to a full harvest.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall with the neck and top half of the bulb above soil level in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; in zone 8–10 amaryllis naturalize in the ground and bloom reliably each winter-spring without the 6–8 week dry rest period required for potted indoor forcing.

directions: Plant bulbs with the top half exposed in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; amaryllis (correctly Hippeastrum) is one of the most spectacularly beautiful of all bulbous plants, producing massive trumpet-shaped flowers 6–8 inches across in brilliant red, pink, white, coral, salmon, or bicolor striped patterns on hollow, leafless stalks 18–24 inches tall. In zone 8–10 amaryllis naturalizes beautifully in the garden, blooming each January through April without any special treatment — simply plant the bulbs in fall, fertilize lightly in fall and early spring, and allow the foliage to die back naturally each summer. Gulf Coast gardens are famous for spectacular outdoor amaryllis displays along entry walks and in foundation plantings. The large clumps multiply year by year to create a more impressive display. Indoor forcing in pots requires a 6–8 week dry rest period at cool temperatures before returning to warmth and water.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in late summer or early fall; once planted, oxblood lily resents disturbance and should be left alone permanently to naturalize. Do not dig or divide unless absolutely necessary.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; the oxblood lily is one of the most beloved and iconic heirloom bulbs of the Gulf South, an old-fashioned schoolhouse garden staple that has been passed along in this region for over 150 years since its introduction from Argentina. The brilliant deep-red trumpet flowers (2–3 per stem) appear suddenly in late August through September, before the leaves, seemingly overnight after the first significant fall rain — hence the alternate name 'hurricane lily.' The strap-like leaves follow in fall and winter, dying back by summer. The bulbs are nearly indestructible in zone 7–9, naturalizing freely and increasing year after year, with established stands producing hundreds of stems in late summer. They thrive in dry, lean conditions and actually perform worse with too much water or fertilizer. A piece of living southern garden history.

common_name: Habranthus (Copper Lily, Rain Lily, Upward-Facing Zephyr Lily)

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; habranthus is distinguished from Zephyranthes by its flowers, which are borne at an upward-angled rather than strictly upright orientation. Plant and then largely ignore — they naturalize and bloom after summer and fall rain events.

directions: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; habranthus (copper lily) is a summer-dormant rain lily that puts on a brief, intense show of large, bright pink trumpet flowers shortly after summer and fall rains, earning the name 'rain lily' for this reliable weather-triggered response. Unlike the straight-up flowers of Zephyranthes, habranthus flowers are borne at a jaunty upward angle, giving them a distinctive, graceful appearance. The flowers are 2–3 inches across, pale to deep rose-pink with a yellow base, and often striped. This species is more vigorous and larger-flowered than most rain lilies. In zone 7–10 the small bulbs naturalize freely to form expanding colonies in lawns and borders. A classic, low-maintenance companion to other Gulf South summer rain lilies (Zephyranthes) with which it can be mixed for a longer combined bloom season.

planting.note: Hang clumps of fresh material on tree branches or structures in any location with bright indirect light and high humidity; Spanish moss requires no soil — it absorbs all moisture and nutrients from the air through tiny scales on its leaves. It grows naturally throughout the Gulf Coast from the Carolinas to Texas.

directions: Hang fresh clumps on tree branches or wire supports in partial shade with good air circulation and humidity; Spanish moss is one of the most distinctive and iconic plants of the American Deep South, the gray-green curtains draping from live oaks and cypresses that define the visual landscape of the Gulf Coast. Despite its name, it is neither a moss nor Spanish — it is an epiphytic bromeliad native from Virginia to Argentina that attaches to tree branches without parasitizing them, absorbing rain, dew, and nutrients from airborne dust through thousands of tiny absorptive scales covering each silvery-gray thread. It provides critical nesting habitat for bats, birds, and jumping spiders, and was historically used as mattress and upholstery stuffing, building insulation, packing material, and for making mud-and-moss wattle walls. It produces tiny, fragrant yellow-green flowers in spring. A piece of living southern heritage for zone 8–11 landscapes.
